query: Pebbles Ai complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Pebble AI's post-mortem on 500 new users and app store issues | Shankar Desai posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: We let 500 more people into Pebble AI last Friday. Here’s the \honest\ post-mortem. What went wrong We sent the wrong email to a single-digit subset of people. A “Welcome to Pebble” note went to those existing users, which created confusion. How'd we respond? Practiced high-quality repair with those users. We owned the error immediately, apologized, and sent them the right info. The harder work was human. We looked each other in the eye, named the miss without defensiveness, and put in patches for next time. What was frustrating Shipping through the app stores reminded me how hard the distribution plumbing still is. Apple and Google each have strengths…and real gaps, from interface to workflow, that I wouldn’t expect from companies with multi-trillion dollars in market cap. If you are a [...] with multi-trillion dollars in market cap. If you are a small team, respect the pipes and budget time for their quirks. What we did well We ran a 24-hour “canary” with our super users before the wider cohort. A few core elements broke, they flagged them for us, and we shipped fixes quickly. It was a dress rehearsal with the house lights on, and it saved hundreds of rough first impressions. Early feedback Feedback on this release is strong from both sides: people who see the before-and-after, and people with fresh eyes. It is still early, so there is still lots of listening to do. The release reminded me that putting something into the world when it is still a seed gives you a pep in your step. Now it’s time for water and sunshine. Some parts will grow, some will wilt, and both will teach [...] Some parts will grow, some will wilt, and both will teach us. If you want to help test Pebble, join the next wave here. It is the last one before we release publicly:

results.title: The Trouble with AI Tribbles – peknet
results.content: Anyone who has been around organizations that depend on software tools knows this truth: your stop-gap, one-off, we-only-intended-this-to-last-a-week software has a high likelihood of sticking around much longer than you intended, to suffer bit rot, become brittle, and break at the least opportune time. [...] People sometimes comment to me on how fast I can write code. My trick? I don’t type or think any faster than anyone else. I simply spend a lot of time getting to know the codebase. Usually I do this by writing tests and fixing things that break. The first few months on the job, as I get to know an existing codebase, I tend to start re-writing it. Not a wholesale replacement. Just small things. All code needs care and weeding, just like a garden. All the good gardeners I know are constantly puttering around, trimming, weeding, moving things around to find a more optimal amount of sunshine, shade or water. It’s the same thing with code. And as I do that, I internalize the structure of the code, which files contain which logic, how all the pieces fit together. [...] The problem with prototypes is sociological: they can work too well. People can quickly become attached to a prototype that actually works and solves a problem. Suddenly your Friday afternoon experiment is a vital piece of your organization’s infrastructure. The people that authorize the paychecks can be hard to convince that we need to stop and write this code as a proper piece of software (tests, documentation, modularity, CI/CD, etc) rather than continue to depend on what was supposed to be a throwaway idea. This is one reason why designers wisely insist on making prototypes ugly during the discovery phase of a project; folks are less likely to attach to something that is unattractive, even if it solves a problem, or at least they are easier to convince later that the prototype was

results.title: AI Apps Keep Breaking? Here's Why
results.content:
That’s why:
 • A prompt that worked yesterday fails today
 • Fixing one thing breaks three others
 • Your “vibe-coded” app becomes fragile and unpredictable

⸻

🧱 The professional fix (no coding required)

Real software isn’t built as one big blob.

It’s built as small, isolated building blocks:
 • Each part does one job
 • Each part is built and tested on its own
 • When something breaks, you fix only that part

AI works the same way.

↘️ Who Am I:
I’m Anto a Principal Engineer with 15+ years of experience building and protecting large-scale multi-million dollar systems for large banks.
I’ve seen what breaks software in the real world, and I can tell you this:

👉 The problem isn’t AI
👉 The problem is building without architecture
